questions.q: What does the analytic index $\ind(D)$ of an elliptic operator $D$ count?
questions.hint: It is a signed count of obstructions: solutions minus obstructions to surjectivity.
questions.explain: $\ind(D)=\dim\ker D-\dim\mathrm{coker}\,D$. Both summands are finite when $D$ is elliptic on a closed manifold; the difference is a deformation-stable integer.

questions.q: Atiyah–Singer asserts that $\ind(D)$ depends only on which datum?
choices: The class of the principal symbol $[\sigma(D)]\in K^0(T^*M)$
questions.hint: Lower-order perturbations leave the index unchanged; only the leading symbol matters.
questions.explain: The index is a homotopy invariant of the symbol; it only sees the K-theory class $[\sigma(D)]\in K^0(T^*M)$, not the operator itself.

questions.q: Why is invariance under deformation a non-trivial fact?
choices: Both $\dim\ker D$ and $\dim\mathrm{coker}\,D$ are individually constant
$\ker D$ may jump under perturbation, but the difference $\dim\ker-\dim\mathrm{coker}$ stays put
The operator $D$ is rigid on closed manifolds
Closed manifolds admit no continuous deformation of operators
questions.hint: Eigenvalues can cross zero; one of $\ker, \mathrm{coker}$ may grow while the other shrinks by the same amount.
questions.explain: Continuous families of Fredholm operators have constant index even when individual kernel and cokernel dimensions jump — the jumps cancel. This stability is what lets the analytic index match a topological invariant.

questions.q: An order-$m$ operator $D=\sum_{|\alpha|\le m}a_\alpha(x)\partial^\alpha$ is elliptic when which condition holds?
choices: Its principal symbol $\sigma_m(D)(x,\xi)=\sum_{|\alpha|=m}a_\alpha(x)(i\xi)^\alpha$ is invertible for every $\xi\ne 0$
questions.hint: Look at the leading-order homogeneous part viewed as a function on $T^*M\setminus 0$.
questions.explain: Ellipticity is the invertibility of the principal symbol away from the zero section of $T^*M$. The Laplacian $-\Delta$ has $\sigma_2=|\xi|^2$, vanishing only at $\xi=0$ — hence elliptic; the heat operator $\partial_t-\Delta$ has $\sigma_2=|\xi|^2$ which vanishes when $\xi=0$ but the time covector is nonzero, making it parabolic, not elliptic.

questions.q: Why does ellipticity force $\dim\ker D<\infty$ and $\dim\mathrm{coker}\,D<\infty$ on a closed manifold?
choices: Sobolev embedding compactly embeds $H^s\hookrightarrow H^{s-1}$, and a parametrix turns $D$ into a Fredholm operator
Eigenvalues of $D$ form a finite set
questions.hint: A parametrix $Q$ inverts $D$ modulo compact errors; Fredholmness then follows.
questions.explain: On a closed manifold, an elliptic operator admits a pseudodifferential parametrix with $QD=I-S$, $DQ=I-T$ where $S,T$ are smoothing (compact between any Sobolev spaces). This identifies $D$ as a Fredholm map between Sobolev spaces.

questions.q: Elliptic regularity says: if $Du=f$ with $f\in H^k$, then …
questions.hint: Solving an order-$m$ equation should let you integrate the equation $m$ times.
questions.explain: Elliptic regularity: $f\in H^k\Rightarrow u\in H^{k+m}$. Iterating, $f$ smooth implies $u$ smooth. Combined with Fredholmness, this means $\ker D$ consists of smooth sections, so the analytic index is finite.

questions.q: What is the K-theory home of the symbol class of an elliptic operator on $M$?
questions.hint: The symbol is a bundle map on $T^*M$, an isomorphism off the zero section.
questions.explain: $\sigma(D)$ defines an iso between pulled-back bundles outside the zero section in $T^*M$, hence a compactly supported K-class $[\sigma(D)]\in K^0(T^*M)$. This is the entry point for the topological index.

questions.q: The topological index is given by which cohomology pairing?
questions.hint: The Todd class corrects for K-orientation when integrating over $T^*M$.
questions.explain: Atiyah–Singer's index formula: $\ind_{\mathrm{top}}(D)=\int_{T^*M}\mathrm{ch}([\sigma(D)])\cdot\mathrm{Td}(TM_\mathbb{C})$. The Todd class is the K-theoretic Thom-class correction translating into ordinary cohomology.

questions.q: Why does the Todd class $\mathrm{Td}$ appear in the formula?
choices: It enforces the K-theory Thom isomorphism on $T^*M$ when translated to ordinary cohomology
It cancels the Pontryagin class of $TM$
It is the Chern character of the trivial bundle
questions.hint: Both $\mathrm{ch}$ and the Thom isomorphism need to be reconciled with each other.
questions.explain: The Riemann–Roch defect: the K-theory pushforward and the ordinary-cohomology pushforward disagree by $\mathrm{Td}(TM_\mathbb{C})$. Matching them is exactly the Riemann–Roch theorem; Atiyah–Singer is its index-theoretic incarnation.

questions.q: Applied to the operator $D=d+d^*\colon\Omega^{\mathrm{even}}\to\Omega^{\mathrm{odd}}$ on a closed oriented manifold, the index theorem recovers …
questions.hint: $\ker(d+d^*)$ on even forms is even-dimensional harmonic cohomology by Hodge theory.
questions.explain: $\ind(d+d^*)=\sum_k(-1)^k\dim H^k(M)=\chi(M)$. The right-hand topological invariant integrates the Pfaffian/Euler form, recovering Gauss–Bonnet–Chern.

questions.q: On a holomorphic vector bundle $E\to X$ over a closed complex manifold, $\ind(\bar\partial_E)$ equals …
questions.hint: Dolbeault cohomology gives the kernels and cokernels of $\bar\partial$ on $(0,k)$-forms with values in $E$.
questions.explain: $\ind(\bar\partial_E)=\sum_k(-1)^k\dim H^k(X,E)$, the holomorphic Euler characteristic. Atiyah–Singer specialises to $\chi(X,E)=\int_X\mathrm{ch}(E)\cdot\mathrm{Td}(TX)$, which is Hirzebruch–Riemann–Roch.

questions.q: The signature theorem identifies $\sigma(M)$ with which integral?
questions.explain: For the signature operator $D_+\colon\Omega^+\to\Omega^-$ on a $4k$-manifold, the index equals the signature: $\sigma(M)=\int_M L(TM)$. In dimension $4$, $\sigma=\tfrac{1}{3}\langle p_1,[M]\rangle$.

questions.q: On a $2n$-dimensional spin manifold, the index of the Dirac operator $D\colon\Gamma(S^+)\to\Gamma(S^-)$ equals …
questions.explain: Atiyah–Singer for the Dirac operator: $\ind(D)=\int_M\hat A(TM)$. On a closed spin $4$-manifold, $\hat A=-p_1/24$, so $\ind(D)=-\langle p_1,[M]\rangle/24$, which equals $-\sigma(M)/8$ via the signature theorem.

questions.q: Lichnerowicz's vanishing theorem says that on a closed spin manifold of positive scalar curvature, …
questions.hint: The Bochner–Weitzenböck formula for $D^2$ has a positive curvature term forcing $\ker D=0$.
questions.explain: Lichnerowicz: $D^2=\nabla^*\nabla+\tfrac{R}{4}$ where $R$ is the scalar curvature. If $R>0$ everywhere, $\ker D=0$, so $\ind(D)=-\dim\mathrm{coker}\,D\le 0$ and dually $\ge 0$, hence $0$. This obstructs many spin manifolds (e.g. $K3$) from carrying positive-scalar-curvature metrics.

questions.q: Why is the integrality of $\hat A(M)$ a non-trivial output on a spin manifold?
choices: $\hat A$ is rational a priori; integrality on spin manifolds comes from $\ind(D)\in\mathbb{Z}$
$\hat A$ is always integer-valued for any oriented manifold
questions.hint: Without spin, $\hat A$ would only land in $\mathbb{Q}$, but $\ind(D)$ is an integer.
questions.explain: The expansion of $\hat A$ involves rational coefficients. The fact that its integral against $[M]$ is an integer when $M$ is spin is one of the first divisibility theorems that the index theorem produces — historically how Rokhlin's $\sigma\equiv 0\pmod{16}$ for spin $4$-manifolds was reproved.

questions.q: The McKean–Singer formula expresses $\ind(D)$ as which trace?
questions.hint: Pair off non-zero eigenvalues of $D^*D$ and $DD^*$; only the kernels survive.
questions.explain: The supertrace of $e^{-tD^2}$ is independent of $t$. As $t\to\infty$ it picks out only $\ker D$ and $\ker D^*=\mathrm{coker}\,D$; as $t\to 0$ the heat-kernel asymptotic produces a local index density. Equating the two limits is the heat-kernel proof.

questions.q: In the families index theorem, the index of a family $\{D_b\}_{b\in B}$ of elliptic operators lives in which group?
questions.hint: Generalise an integer to a virtual bundle on the parameter space.
questions.explain: The kernels and cokernels assemble into a virtual bundle $[\ker D]-[\mathrm{coker}\,D]\in K^0(B)$. The families version of Atiyah–Singer computes this via a fibre-integration of $\mathrm{ch}\cdot\mathrm{Td}$.

questions.q: In gauge theory, the index of the chiral Dirac operator coupled to a connection $A$ on a $4$-manifold corresponds physically to …
choices: The instanton number / second Chern number of the gauge field
The mass of the lightest fermion
questions.explain: $\ind(D_A)=\int_M\hat A(TM)\cdot\mathrm{ch}(E_A)$ — for a $\mathrm{SU}(N)$ bundle in dim $4$ the leading piece is $\int_M c_2(E_A)$, the instanton number. The chiral anomaly in physics is exactly this index.
